title: Post-seismic response of the outer accretionary prism after the 2010 Maule earthquake, Chile
abstract: Ten ocean-bottom seismometers were deployed from May 2012 to March 2013 in the outer accretionary prism updip from the greatest slip of the 2010 Maule earthquake. Transient seafloor pressure changes were observed on absolute pressure gauges with polarity changes suggesting shallow source depths, and their duration of hours to days was shorter than most geodetic transients from onshore GPS networks.

location.description: Outer accretionary prism offshore central Chile (~35°S); OBS passive deployment monitoring post-Maule earthquake deformation

acquisition.vessel: R/V Melville (AT26-09 cruise, 2012) and recovery cruise (2013)

data.name: Maule Post-Seismic OBS Passive Array — Outer Accretionary Prism ~35°S (2012–2013)
data.description: 10 OBS with absolute pressure gauges deployed May 2012–March 2013 in the outer accretionary prism at ~35°S; monitors post-seismic deformation and transient pressure changes after the 2010 Maule earthquake

_data_note: Passive OBS deployment (no active source). Data may be at IRIS/EarthScope. AT26-09 cruise (same as Tréhu 2019).

processing.summary: Ten ocean-bottom seismometers equipped with absolute pressure gauges were deployed from May 2012 to March 2013 in the outer accretionary prism updip from maximum Maule earthquake slip (~35°S). The passive records document transient pressure changes on timescales of hours to days, interpreted as shallow post-seismic deformation. No active seismic source used.
workflow: 2. Clock correction and instrument response removal
processing.notes: Passive OBS study; companion to Tréhu et al. 2019 MCS survey in same area

analysis_notes: Geosphere paper; behind paywall. Passive OBS data possibly at IRIS. No active-source MCS in this paper.
